label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the Indians believed according to the letter they wrote refusing the offer to send their boys to William and Mary College. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to recall the content of the letter. The letter says that the officials from Maryland and Virginia wanted the Six Nations Indians to send their boys to the college. The Indians declined, explaining that they know the officials mean well, but they have different ways of looking at things. They mention that some of their young men went to northern colleges, learned all the sciences, but when they came back, they had bad manners and couldn't live in the woods anymore—they were "totally good for nothing." Then the Indians offer a counter-proposal: if the Virginia gentlemen send their sons, the Indians would take care of their education and make men of them. So the key points here are that the Indians are refusing the offer because they value a different kind of education. They don't think the college education is suitable for their young people. They believe that their own methods are better. The letter emphasizes that different nations have different ways of looking at things, and their experience shows that the Western education isn't working for their youth. Looking at the options: A) It would be better for their boys to receive some schooling. But the Indians are refusing the offer, so this doesn't fit. They believe that the offered schooling is not suitable. B) They were being insulted by the offer. No, the letter is polite. They thank the officials and say they mean to do good. They don't seem insulted, just that the offer isn't right for them. C) They knew more about science than the officials. The letter doesn't mention that. They actually say the officials have a high opinion of their sciences, and the problem is not about scientific knowledge but about the way of life and manners. D) They had a better way of educating young men. This fits with the letter's argument. They
